abstract.text: This article describes the KeyboardKit autocomplete engine.

inlineContent.text: Autocomplete is an important part of the typing experience, where suggestions are shown as the user types.
In KeyboardKit, an
can provide suggestions to an
whenever the user types. Unlike other service types, there is no open-source implementation of this protocol.
unlocks local and remote autocomplete. Information about Pro features can be found at the end of this article.
KeyboardKit has an
namespace that contains autocomplete-related types, models and views.
KeyboardKit has an observable
that handles autocomplete state, such as if autocomplete is enabled, which suggestions to present, etc.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of this class, injects it into
and updates it whenever autocomplete is performed.
KeyboardKit has an observable
that handles autocomplete settings, such as how many suggestions to display, whether or not autocomplete and autocorrect is enabled, etc.

KeyboardKit’s settings types are observable and will automatically persist any changes made. KeyboardKit will observe these types and sync any changes made to them to their respective contexts, which will automatically update the affected views.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of this class and injects it into
. You can read more about how settings are handled in the
and
articles.
In KeyboardKit, an
can provide suggestions when the user types or the text input cursor moves in the text.
KeyboardKit doesn’t have a standard autocomplete service implementation. Instead, it injects a disabled service into
until you replace it with your own implementation or register
KeyboardKit will automatically call
whenever the keyboard text changes, then update the
context with suggestions from the
has many open autocomplete-related functions that can be used to configure how it performs autocomplete, which text it uses, etc. You can also use the
to configure how autocomplete behaves.
However, ieverything eventually comes down to which
you use. The
in
will honor all supported configurations, while
will not.
Views like the autocomplete
can list these suggestions and handle suggestion with the main
You can override any autocomplete-related property or function in
, such as
or
, to customize how autocomplete is handled by the controller.
You can also disable autocorrection by applying a
view modifier to the keyboard view.
You can use the
to perform changes that are not persisted, such as using
to enable or disable autocomplete & autocorrect,
, etc.
also has an
that can be used to add custom autocorrect text replacements, which for instance will be used by the
You can use the
to perform changes that will automatically persist, and  automatically update the context. Settings can also sync between the main app and keyboard extensions. See
for more information.

content.text: How to create a custom service

inlineContent.text: You can create a custom autocomplete service to customize the autocomplete behavior, to integrate with 3rd party tools, etc. You can implement
from scratch, or inherit and customize any of the
services.
For instance, here’s a custom service that just adds a suffix to the current word:

code: func hasIgnoredWord(_ word: String) -> Bool { false }
func hasLearnedWord(_ word: String) -> Bool { false }
func fakeSuggestion(_ text: String, _ subtitle: String? = nil) -> Autocomplete.Suggestion {

inlineContent.text: To use this service instead of the standard one, just inject it into
This will make KeyboardKit use your custom service instead of the standard one.
The
namespace has autocomplete-specific views, that can be used to mimic native autocomplete toolbars and items.
The autocomplete
mimics a native autocomplete toolbar, and can be used to present autocomplete suggestions to the user:
This view can be styled with a
, which can be applied with the
view modifier. It can also use custom
views.
The autocomplete-specific
and
views can be used to customize the autocomplete
These views can be styled with the
and
styles, which can be applied with the
and
view modifiers.
unlocks additional autocomplete services and injects a
into
when you register a valid license key. The pro services are all open to inheritance, so you can inherit and customize them to fit your needs.
The
performs autocomplete operations locally, on-device. It supporst most keyboard locales, works offline, doesn’t require Full Access and can integrate with system services, like the local lexicon.
This service currently doesn’t provide next word prediction, since Apple removed these capabilities in iOS 16.
The
can be used to perform autocomplete by integrating with an external API. It requires that the device is online, and requires that Full Access is enabled, to allow it to make network requests.
The
supports the following locales:
To support other locales, you must use a remote autocomplete service or create a custom service implementation.
